paragraphs.text: While we share the view that many of the issues raised in the consultation are important from a consumer-protection perspective, our assessment is that virtually all of these areas are already covered by existing legislation (see attached table).

section_path: New detailed legislation risks being less effective than active enforcement of existing rules

evidence_quotes.quote: more active application of the rules already in force.
complementary guidelines could play an important role in providing clarity.

paragraphs.text: In the vast majority of cases, the challenges highlighted by the Commission in the consultation can be addressed through more active application of the rules already in force. In some instances, complementary guidelines could play an important role in providing clarity. It is therefore NDM’s view that new detailed legislation is not necessary at this time.

section_path: New detailed legislation risks being less effective than active enforcement of existing rules

paragraphs.text: As with the earlier consultation on the Digital Fairness Check, we also wish to point out that this consultation is leading in its design. The wording gives the impression that the Commission has already reached the conclusion that further regulation of the highlighted business practices is needed, and the design of the consultation steers respondents in that direction.

section_path: New detailed legislation risks being less effective than active enforcement of existing rules

paragraphs.text: A good method, in NDM’s view, would be to approach these issues in an open and exploratory manner. Our view is that such a method would have shown that the main problems lie in shortcomings in enforcement and the subsequent lack of case law. In this context, it should also be noted that new legislation risks sweeping away the limited body of case law that exists today.

section_path: New detailed legislation risks being less effective than active enforcement of existing rules

paragraphs.text: Generally, detailed legislation is an inefficient way to address the challenges/areas identified in the consultation. These areas evolve rapidly, which means that detailed rules risk becoming outdated quickly as technology and new methods in, among other things, marketing and digital communications develop.

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: We therefore note that a Digital Fairness Act, in the form suggested by the consultation, would be in direct conflict with the Commission’s stated simplification objectives. From a simplification perspective, there is a significant risk that any Digital Fairness Act would lead to overlaps with existing legislation, which in turn would create legal uncertainty for European businesses (especially as new detailed rules become outdated). This would be incompatible with the Commission’s ambition to stimulate growth by reducing overregulation and regulatory uncertainty.

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: The work on a Digital Fairness Act was initiated under the previous Commission, which placed less emphasis on simplification. It is not unlikely that, had a Digital Fairness Act been adopted then, it would already be the subject of simplification discussions and an Omnibus initiative.

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: A far better approach from a simplification perspective would be to intensify the application of existing rules and, where needed, develop guidelines for consistent enforcement.

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: One possible way forward would be, as a first step, to launch an initiative to follow up on and assess the extent to which national supervisory authorities are in fact using the existing toolbox – especially the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD). Such a follow-up exercise would provide a clearer picture of any need for targeted guidelines and for further case law. Such an approach would likely achieve the goals highlighted in the consultation in a more effective and relevant manner than new detailed legislation, while allowing the rules to keep pace with developments in technology and market practice.

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: Yours sincerely,

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: Mattias Grundström Chairman NDM

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: NDM

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: The Stockholm based Business Delegation (NDM) consists of 24 Swedish industry and interest organizations. Through NDM, they work to promote fair rules of play, equitable competition, and high standards of consumer and privacy protection in marketing, including sales activities.

section_path: New detailed regulation would conflict with the Commission’s simplification ambition

paragraphs.text: This document is not a comprehensive legal opinion or formal legal advice. It is a high-level overview of how the issues raised in the Commission's consultation of a Digital Fairness Act are currently addressed under EU consumer and digital frameworks (e.g., UCPD, UCTD, CRD, Price Indication Directive, GDPR/ePrivacy, DSA/AVMSD) and selected national implementations. The analysis is illustrative, may omit nuances or exceptions, and does not account for all sector-specific or Member-State rules, guidance, or case law. Laws and enforcement practices can change rapidly and may apply differently to specific facts.

section_path: Table of relationship between DFA consultation and existing EU law.

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns are unfair commercial practices deployed through the design of digital interfaces that can influence consumers to take decisions they would not have taken otherwise.

paragraphs.text: "Dark patterns are unfair commercial practices deployed through the design of digital interfaces that can influence consumers to take decisions they would not have taken otherwise. Examples of such practices may include but are not limited to: presenting choices in a leading manner (e.g. trader's preferred choice in colour, prominently displayed, other option(s) in black and white and difficult to find), using countdown timers to create urgency or asking misleading questions using double negatives."

section_path: Section 1 – Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: “Presenting choices in a leading manner” (trader’s preferred choice highlighted in colour/size; other choices greyed out/hidden).
“Other option(s) difficult to find”

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | “Presenting choices in a leading manner” (trader’s preferred choice highlighted in colour/size; other choices greyed out/hidden). | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), arts. 6–7 (incl. art. 7(2) on clarity and prominence). | The design makes alternative, material choices less visible → skews the decision basis. Lack of professional diligence, misleading act/omission. | | | | | | “Other option(s) difficult to find” | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), art 7 | Material information/alternatives are withheld or presented unclearly. Lack of professional diligence, misleading omission. |

section_path: Section 1 – Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: Using countdown timers to create urgency
Misleading questions using double negatives

If scarcity is false/exaggerated: misleading. B) True claim used in a way that unduly pressures the decision. If true but pressuring: aggressive practice. Lack of professional diligence | | | | | | “Misleading questions using double negatives” | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 6–7 | Ambiguous wording likely makes the consumer choose something they did not intend. Misleading, lack of professional diligence. |

section_path: Section 1 – Dark Patterns

paragraphs.text: Section 1 – question 2

section_path: Section 1 – Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: Click fatigue (many steps to make the “right” choice)
Friction that reduces consumer freedom of action in checkout/cancellation.
Creating the impression no other choice exists (other choices hidden/minimised)

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Click fatigue (many steps to make the “right” choice) | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), arts. 8–9. | Friction that reduces consumer freedom of action in checkout/cancellation. Aggressive (pressure, obstacles), lack of professional diligence. | | | | | | Creating the impression no other choice exists (other choices hidden/minimised) | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 6–7 | “False hierarchy”/hidden alternative path. Misleading omission possibly act as well. |

section_path: Section 1 – Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: Nagging (repeated prompts to choose a particular option)
Urgency/scarcity (countdown, “few left”) when the offer is in fact time-/stock-limited
Urgency/scarcity when the claim is false/exaggerated
Confirm-shaming (shaming language on decline/cancellation).
“Sneak into basket” (adding an item/service without an explicit choice).

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Nagging (repeated prompts to choose a particular option) | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 8-9. | Persistent prompts may amount to undue influence. Aggressive practice | | Urgency/scarcity (countdown, “few left”) when the offer is in fact time-/stock-limited | UCPD art 8-9 | True claim, but the way it is used can amount to undue influence. Aggressive practice (design pressures even though the claim is true) | | Urgency/scarcity when the claim is false/exaggerated | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 6–7 | False or incomplete information about time/stock. Misleading act. | | Confirm-shaming (shaming language on decline/cancellation). | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 8-9. | Emotional pressure affecting the decision. Aggressive practice. | | “Sneak into basket” (adding an item/service without an explicit choice). | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 6–7, p 29 of annex I. CRD art 22 and 27. | Prohibited regardless of effect; often at the final checkout step. Blacklisted practice / inertia selling; also misleading. CRD art. 22 (bans pre-ticked boxes/extra payments without active choice) and art 27 says consumer doesn’t have to pay. |

section_path: Section 1 – Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: Function producing a different outcome than expected (e.g., “cancel” leads to sales page)
Ambiguous wording (e.g., double negatives)
Leading presentation (e.g., vivid colour/larger font for trader-preferred option)

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Function producing a different outcome than expected (e.g., “cancel” leads to sales page) | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), art. 6 and arts. 8-9. | Mismatch between label and function; may also hinder cancellation. Misleading and aggressive as it hinders cancellation. | | | | | | Ambiguous wording (e.g., double negatives) | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 6–7. | Unclear/ambiguous info in decision-critical steps. Misleading act. | | | | | | Leading presentation (e.g., vivid colour/larger font for trader-preferred option) | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), UCPD arts. 6–7. | If other choices are obscured/demoted so that the choice is skewed. Misleading, lack of professional diligence. |

section_path: Section 1 – Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: Addictive design features in digital products are those that make consumers spend more time and money online than intended,
recommender systems that are steered to increase the consumer’s engagement.

paragraphs.text: Addictive design features in digital products are those that make consumers spend more time and money online than intended, e.g. infinite scrolling (where a page loads content with no distinct end), content that disappears quickly (ephemeral stories), autoplay (that allows video or audio files to play without user’s intervention), applying penalties for disengagement (such as breaking a streak) or recommender systems that are steered to increase the consumer’s engagement.

section_path: Section 2 – Addictive design

evidence_quotes.quote: Infinite scroll/ephemeral stories/autoplay that funnels into purchase flow or makes cancellation harder
“Streaks”/penalties for disengagement linked to purchases (e.g., buy coins to save the streak).
Recommender systems steering towards paid or more expensive choices.

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Infinite scroll/ephemeral stories/autoplay that funnels into purchase flow or makes cancellation harder | UCPD arts. 8-9 | Aggressive practice (friction/persistence/undue pressure). | | | | | | “Streaks”/penalties for disengagement linked to purchases (e.g., buy coins to save the streak). | UCPD arts. 8-9 and 6-7. | Aggressive practice. misleading if consequences/price are obscured | | | | | | Recommender systems steering towards paid or more expensive choices. | UCPD arts. 6-7 and 8-9. | Misleading by omission/presentation; possibly aggressive if it creates pressure. |

section_path: Section 2 – Addictive design

evidence_quotes.quote: Time/scarcity nudges (“offer ends in 2 minutes”) in checkout/cancellation.

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Time/scarcity nudges (“offer ends in 2 minutes”) in checkout/cancellation. | UCPD arts. 6-7 and 8-9. | A) False scarcity → Misleading. B) True scarcity → may still be aggressive if the design pressures. |

section_path: Section 2 – Addictive design

evidence_quotes.quote: target attention and behaviour more than a specific purchase decision.

paragraphs.text: Comment: Section 2 is not primarily about consumer protection (i.e., guarding transactional decisions). Rather, it is about protecting users' well-being, autonomy, and especially minors, and about platform governance. The listed features—infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks, engagement-optimised recommenders—target attention and behaviour more than a specific purchase decision.

section_path: Section 2 – Addictive design

evidence_quotes.quote: If such features are tied to a buy/renew/cancel moment they fall inside classic unfair-commercial-practice control.

paragraphs.text: If such features are tied to a buy/renew/cancel moment they fall inside classic unfair-commercial-practice control.

section_path: Section 2 – Addictive design

evidence_quotes.quote: mitigating psychological pressure, preventing over-engagement

paragraphs.text: When such features are not tied to a buy/renew/cancel moment, they fall outside classic unfair-commercial-practice control and align instead with digital-environment safeguards: mitigating psychological pressure, preventing over-engagement, and managing systemic risks to minors and public discourse.

section_path: Section 2 – Addictive design

evidence_quotes.quote: uncertainty-based rewards
imitating gambling (e.g. loot boxes)
pay-to-progress and pay-to-win mechanisms
in-app purchases offered in exchange for virtual currencies that blur the real-world value of those transactions.

paragraphs.text: Specific concerns have arisen with regard to certain features in digital products, for example in-app purchases that include uncertainty-based rewards, imitating gambling (e.g. loot boxes), pay-to-progress and pay-to-win mechanisms, and in-app purchases offered in exchange for virtual currencies that blur the real-world value of those transactions.

section_path: Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games

evidence_quotes.quote: Loot boxes / uncertainty-based rewards.
AVMSD (advertising to minors). Misleading by omission about randomness and probabilities/odds; possibly misleading claims in marketing (“increased chance”, “exclusive”).

section_path: Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games

evidence_quotes.quote: how their personal data are used to personalise commercial offers

paragraphs.text: Consumers often find personalised offers and content useful (e.g. personalised advertising). At the same time, many consumers are concerned about how their personal data are used to personalise commercial offers, and many have the impression that information about their vulnerabilities (e.g. personal problems, financial challenges, or negative mental states) is used unfairly for commercial purposes.

section_path: Section 4 – Unfair personalisation practices

evidence_quotes.quote: Must it be disclosed that the price is personalised?
Recital 45 resp. Art 4 (4) (ii) of Directive 2019/2161 (Omnibus) – CRD explicit disclosure of personalised pricing in online stores.
Use of vulnerabilities to influence consumer.

The exploitation by the trader of any specific misfortune or circumstance of such gravity as to impair the consumer's judgement, of which the trader is aware, to influence the consumer's decision with regard to the product is considered aggressive practice, Lack of professional diligence.
The profiling itself is covered by GDPR – UCPD covers the result of the profiling -> the offer presented to the consumer.

UCPD art 7 misleading omission of legal information. | Recital 45 resp. GDPR on profiling. section_path: Section 4 – Unfair personalisation practices

evidence_quotes.quote: hidden marketing

paragraphs.text: With the increasing importance of social media for consumer transactions, reports of problematic commercial practices have become more prominent. Concerns arise with regard to social media influencers, for example as regards hidden marketing and the promotion and sale of potentially harmful products.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: Hidden advertising / unclear sponsorship.
Misleading by omission; advertorial misleading about the commercial purposes.
Direct purchase prompts addressed to children.
Marketing/linking to illegal products.
Marketing/linking to products with 18+ restriction.

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Hidden advertising / unclear sponsorship. | UCPD art 5 (2) (a), art. 7; Annex I pt 11. AVMSD (identification), DSA (ad transparency), industry codes setting level of professional diligence. | Misleading by omission; advertorial misleading about the commercial purposes. | | | | | | Direct purchase prompts addressed to children. | UCPD Annex I pt 28. | Blacklisted practice | | | | | | Marketing/linking to illegal products. | UCPD Annex I pt 9. | Blacklisted: claiming/giving impression a product can be sold legally when it cannot. | | | | | | Marketing/linking to products with 18+ restriction. | UCPD art 5 (2) (a) industry codes setting level of professional diligence. | Social responsibility etc. in the ICC-code. |

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: including labelling/identification duties and sector-specific rules.
determining whether a post/story/stream is an "advertisement"

paragraphs.text: Comment: Once content is advertising, UCPD governs unfair practices; however, determining whether a post/story/stream is an "advertisement" is primarily an AVMSD matter (and for national media regulators to operationalise), including labelling/identification duties and sector-specific rules.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: when influencer content qualifies as "advertising" versus editorial/personal expression

paragraphs.text: The boundary question—when influencer content qualifies as "advertising" versus editorial/personal expression—cannot be resolved within the scope of a DFA as it will be a case by case assessment of the content and the DFA would in practice only replace UCPD in this assessment.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: truthfulness, clarity, absence of undue pressure

paragraphs.text: As for "potentially harmful products", products are either lawful or unlawful to place on the market. If a product is unlawful, any marketing is out of bounds. If a product is lawful, its marketing is in principle permitted; what EU consumer law (UCPD/CRD) polices is how it is marketed (truthfulness, clarity, absence of undue pressure), not whether it may be marketed at all.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: limits on what can be promoted, to whom, where, and how.

paragraphs.text: Restrictions on marketing lawful products arise only where specific sectoral rules apply (e.g., medicinal products, tobacco/nicotine, alcohol, food/health claims) or where Member States adopt proportionate, non-discriminatory limits for public-health or similar overriding reasons. In other words, Section 5 should distinguish clearly between (i) illegality of the product (market access question) and (ii) lawful products where marketing is allowed unless targeted restrictions—often grounded in public health and typically within Member State competence—justify limits on what can be promoted, to whom, where, and how.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: drip pricing (failing to disclose upfront mandatory and unavoidable costs and fees and adding them later in the course of the booking)
advertising attractive “starting” prices whilst automatically applying dynamic price increases (rendering such starting prices unrealistic for a majority of buyers)
price comparisons based on vague reference prices that give a false impression of reduction of the selling price.

paragraphs.text: Consumers may face unfair practices related to the marketing of the price, such as drip pricing (failing to disclose upfront mandatory and unavoidable costs and fees and adding them later in the course of the booking), advertising attractive “starting” prices whilst automatically applying dynamic price increases (rendering such starting prices unrealistic for a majority of buyers) and misleading practices regarding price comparisons based on vague reference prices that give a false impression of reduction of the selling price.

section_path: Section 6 – Unfair marketing related to pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: Drip pricing – mandatory/ unavoidable fees only revealed late in booking
“From-price” plus automatic dynamic increases making the starting price unrealistic for most.
Misleading price comparisons – vague reference prices giving a false impression of reduction.

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Drip pricing – mandatory/ unavoidable fees only revealed late in booking | UCPD art. 7 (incl. 7(2) on clarity/prominence and 7(4) for invitation to purchase), possibly art. 6. CRD art. 6(1)(e) (total price incl. taxes/fees), CRD art. 8(2) (clear price info at the order button); sector rules (e.g., aviation: final price from first display). | Misleading omission (material price info missing or unclear); possibly misleading act if “total price” is claimed to be lower. | | | | | | “From-price” plus automatic dynamic increases making the starting price unrealistic for most. | UCPD arts. 6 and 7; Annex I pt 5 (bait advertising). Directive 98/6/EC (price indications) on clear sales price; CRD Art. 6(1) (clear, intelligible pre-contract info) | Show data on actual availability at the “from-price” (share of sessions/customers who can buy at it), applicable conditions and if speed of price increases in a typical journey. Misleading act and/or omission; in hard cases may resemble bait advertising. | | | | | | Misleading price comparisons – vague reference prices giving a false impression of reduction. | UCPD arts. 6 and 7. 98/6/EC art. 6a (Omnibus): when announcing a price reduction, the reference is normally the lowest price in the previous 30 days (exceptions for perishable goods). | Show documentation of the reference price source/method; how the comparison was presented (“previous price”, “recommended price”, etc.). |

section_path: Section 6 – Unfair marketing related to pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: cancellation or renewal of subscriptions
the conversion of free trials into paid subscriptions

paragraphs.text: Consumers may face issues with digital contracts, notably regarding the cancellation or renewal of subscriptions, or the conversion of free trials into paid subscriptions, and with automated contracts. In addition, consumers may not have the possibility to contact a person when trying to reach the customer service about their contract.

section_path: Section 7 – Issues with digital contracts

evidence_quotes.quote: Auto-renewal without clear disclosure at conclusion.
Renewal/cancellation via nudge/urgency (countdown, “last chance to keep”) where the claim is true.
"Automated contracts” (fully automated contracting where UI/texts create the wrong impression).
Misleading price/fees in cancellation or renewal flow (e.g., hidden cancellation fees).
No way to reach a human in customer service about the contract.

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Auto-renewal without clear disclosure at conclusion. | UCPD arts. 6–7. CRD Art. 6(1) (contract terms incl. duration/renewal). UCTD (unfair terms) | Misleading omission/act (material contract term omitted/downplayed. | | | | | | Renewal/cancellation via nudge/urgency (countdown, “last chance to keep”) where the claim is true. | UCPD arts. 8–9. CRD (clarity on terms/price before renewal). | Aggressive practice if the design exerts undue pressure. | | | | | | Misleading price/fees in cancellation or renewal flow (e.g., hidden cancellation fees). | UCPD arts. 6–7. CRD Art. 6(1)(e) (total price incl. fees). | Misleading act/omission. | | | | | | “Automated contracts” (fully automated contracting where UI/texts create the wrong impression). | UCPD arts. 6–7. CRD Art. 6(1) (pre-contract info), CRD Art. 8(2) (clear payment confirmation), UCTD (unfairness). | Misleading about contract steps, price or terms. | | | | | | No way to reach a human in customer service about the contract. | UCPD art 5 (2) (a) and arts 6-7 and 8-9. Especially UCPD art 6 (1)(b) (after-sale customer assistance and complaint handling). | Aggressive/lack of professional diligence if it effectively prevents exercising legal rights (e.g., cancellation/withdrawal). Misleading about the handling of after-sale customer assistance and complaint handling. |

section_path: Section 7 – Issues with digital contracts

evidence_quotes.quote: Difficult cancellation (“cancellation labyrinth”, redirected cancel button, no clear path).
para. 1(h) – automatic renewal where the deadline for the consumer to oppose renewal is unreasonably early;

paragraphs.text: | Described problem | Covered by existing rules? | Short justification | | --- | --- | --- | | Difficult cancellation (“cancellation labyrinth”, redirected cancel button, no clear path). | UCPD arts. 8–9 (pressure/obstacles), art. 7(2) (clear and prominent info), possibly art. 5 (professional diligence). | UCPD arts. 8–9 (pressure/obstacles), art. 7(2) (clear and prominent info), possibly art. 5 (professional diligence). | | | CRD Art. 6(1) (pre-contract info), CRD Arts. 9–11 (withdrawal/consequences), for platforms also DSA Art. 25 (cancellation not harder than sign-up). | CRD Art. 6(1) (pre-contract info), CRD Arts. 9–11 (withdrawal/consequences), for platforms also DSA Art. 25 (cancellation not harder than sign-up). | | | Directive 93/13/EEC on Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts (UCTD) | UCTD annex (indicative list of terms which may be regarded as unfair), in particular: | | | Art 3 (significant imbalance contrary to the requirement of good faith) and | para. 1(h) – automatic renewal where the deadline for the consumer to oppose renewal is unreasonably early; | | | Art 5 (plain and intelligible language). | para. 1(g) – enabling the seller or supplier to terminate an open-ended contract without reasonable notice; | | | Unclear or unbalanced termination or renewal clauses may be declared void. | para. 1(l) – allowing price increases without a corresponding right for the consumer to terminate the contract; and paras. 1(d), 1(f), 1(q) concerning, inter alia, imbalance upon cancellation, unilateral termination rights, and hindrances to the consumer’s legal remedies. |

section_path: Section 7 – Issues with digital contracts

evidence_quotes.quote: a clause found to be unfair in an injunction procedure is not binding on consumers

paragraphs.text: Comment: According to the case law of the Court of Justice of the European Union, Invitel (C-472/10), a clause found to be unfair in an injunction procedure is not binding on consumers — including those who were not parties to the proceedings — and that the effects apply ex tunc (from the time the contract was concluded) with regard to the clause itself.

section_path: Section 7 – Issues with digital contracts

paragraphs.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
